rationale: AI-Scientist v2 performs literature search during ideation, not as a separate novelty gate. Treat this as provisional until a separate checker is added.

raw.Name: uncertainty_aware_cfg
raw.Title: Uncertainty-Aware Classifier-Free Guidance for Diffusion Sampling
raw.Short Hypothesis: Classifier-free guidance is currently controlled by fixed or timestep-only schedules, but denoiser reliability also varies across individual samples. The hypothesis is that a simple internal uncertainty signal—computed from disagreement under lightweight stochastic perturbations of the denoiser—can identify when conditional guidance should be trusted or damped. Using this signal to adapt guidance strength per sample and timestep will improve the fidelity-diversity tradeoff over fixed CFG and over purely timestep-based schedules. This is the right setting because the question is specifically about local guidance reliability during iterative denoising; offline global tuning cannot reveal whether guidance should change for a particular sample at a particular step.
raw.Related Work: Prior work on classifier-free guidance mainly uses fixed scales or hand-designed timestep schedules. Recent work such as Adaptive Classifier-Free Guidance via Dynamic Low-Confidence Masking (2025) adapts guidance using confidence, but in masked diffusion language models and by modifying the unconditional input rather than estimating denoiser uncertainty in continuous image diffusion. Annealed or learned guidance schedules in image diffusion also adapt over time, but they are primarily timestep- or signal-based, not sample-specific uncertainty estimates derived from denoiser disagreement. More broadly, uncertainty estimation via dropout or ensembles is well known in deep learning, and adaptive solvers exist for diffusion sampling, but they are not typically used to modulate semantic guidance strength itself. The key distinction here is a simple, training-light method for image diffusion that uses internal denoiser disagreement at inference time to control CFG locally for each sample and step.
raw.Abstract: Classifier-free guidance (CFG) is one of the most effective tools for improving conditional diffusion models, but its guidance scale is usually fixed across all samples or scheduled only as a function of timestep. This ignores an important fact: the denoiser is not equally reliable everywhere along the reverse trajectory. We propose Uncertainty-Aware CFG, a simple inference-time method that estimates local denoiser uncertainty and uses it to modulate guidance strength per sample and per timestep. Our approach computes a cheap disagreement signal from two or more low-cost stochastic predictions, such as Monte Carlo dropout or tiny latent perturbations, and maps that signal to an adaptive guidance scale. When the denoiser is confident, guidance is increased to improve condition alignment; when uncertainty is high, guidance is reduced to avoid oversaturation, semantic drift, and mode collapse. The method requires no external classifier and can be added to existing diffusion samplers with minimal code changes. We will evaluate the approach on lightweight class-conditional and text-conditional image benchmarks, comparing against fixed CFG and recent dynamic guidance baselines. The main questions are whether uncertainty is predictive of guidance failure, whether adaptive local control improves fidelity at matched diversity, and whether the method can recover some of the benefits of extensive guidance tuning automatically. If successful, this work would provide a simple and practical principle for diffusion sampling: guide strongly only when the denoiser appears locally trustworthy.
Experiments: 1. Implement two lightweight uncertainty proxies for image diffusion sampling: (a) Monte Carlo dropout, using two stochastic denoiser evaluations at selected timesteps, and (b) perturbation disagreement, using two denoiser predictions from slightly perturbed noisy latents. Define uncertainty as the norm of disagreement in predicted noise or x0. Map uncertainty to an adaptive CFG scale via a monotone rule such as w_t = w_min + (w_max - w_min) * exp(-beta * u_t).

2. Evaluate on small academic-scale benchmarks: CIFAR-10 class-conditional diffusion, ImageNet-64 if feasible, and a low-resolution latent text-to-image setup or subset of MS-COCO. Compare fixed CFG, a simple timestep-dependent annealed CFG baseline, and the proposed uncertainty-aware CFG. Metrics: FID, precision/recall, Inception Score for class-conditional tasks, CLIP score for text alignment, and LPIPS-based diversity.

3. Test whether uncertainty predicts guidance failure. For a grid of fixed CFG scales, measure when samples show oversaturation, artifact growth, or diversity loss. Correlate these failures with uncertainty estimates at each timestep. Report AUROC or rank correlation for predicting poor final samples from early or mid-trajectory uncertainty.

4. Ablate the uncertainty source and update frequency. Compare dropout versus perturbation disagreement, evaluate uncertainty every step versus every k steps, and measure the tradeoff between quality gains and added NFE. This determines whether the method remains practical under tight compute budgets.

5. Study mode coverage under strong conditioning. Use class-imbalanced subsets or compositional prompts to test whether uncertainty-aware CFG preserves more diversity than aggressive fixed CFG at similar fidelity. Metrics: recall, class coverage, and prompt-attribute success rate.

6. Run a toy 2D experiment on Gaussian mixtures or spiral data with a learned score model. Visualize where uncertainty rises along trajectories and show that reducing guidance in those regions avoids collapse toward a subset of modes. This provides an interpretable mechanistic figure for the paper.
Risk Factors and Limitations: A closely related literature on dynamic or confidence-based CFG may grow quickly, so novelty depends on clearly focusing on image diffusion and internal denoiser-disagreement uncertainty rather than token masking or purely learned schedules.
Dropout-based uncertainty may be noisy or require model modifications if dropout is absent; perturbation disagreement is more universal but may be less calibrated.
Repeated denoiser calls increase sampling cost, so the method must show clear gains at small overhead or use sparse uncertainty updates.
Uncertainty may mostly correlate with timestep, in which case a simple global schedule could capture much of the benefit and weaken the claim of sample-specific adaptation.
Benefits may be modest on easy datasets like CIFAR-10; stronger validation may require at least one moderately challenging conditional benchmark.
The method improves sampling behavior but does not solve training-time deficiencies in the diffusion model itself.
